figures.gloss: The central entity of the tradition, revered not as a literary symbol, but as a genuine, sentient extraterrestrial or spiritual intelligence. He is the adversary of orthodox creation, offering humanity the tools for self-liberation, knowledge, and spiritual evolution.
figures.facet: Unlike secular Satanism, he is a real entity to be communed with; unlike the Christian framework, he is the benevolent initiator rather than the source of arbitrary evil or degradation.
figures.provenance: Michael W. Ford, 'The Bible of the Adversary' (2007); S. Connolly's Demonolatry framework; Temple of the Black Light, 'Liber Azerate' (2002) and the broader anti-cosmic literature.

figures.gloss: The primal mother of demons and the first wife of Adam, who fled Eden rather than submit to male dominance. She is venerated as a distinct goddess of witchcraft, sexual autonomy, and the mysteries of the dark feminine.
figures.facet: She represents the ultimate refusal of subservience. In modern practice, she is a patroness of bodily autonomy and shadow-work, entirely detached from her ancient mythological role as a mere child-stealing night terror.
figures.provenance: Alphabet of Sirach (reinterpreted); Michael W. Ford, 'The Bible of the Adversary' (2007); Left-Hand Path occultism.

figures.gloss: The isolate intelligence or divine spark granted to humanity by Satan, distinguishing humans from mere biological animals. It is the locus of free will, self-awareness, and the capacity for magical manipulation of reality.
figures.facet: Its cultivation is the core of the Left-Hand Path. The goal is never to merge this flame back into a universal, homogenous fire, but to feed it until the individual becomes a sovereign star.
figures.provenance: Originated as philosophical metaphor by Anton LaVey, repurposed as a literal spiritual reality by Michael Aquino (the 'Gift of Set').

figures.gloss: A formal, deeply personal ritual of dedication to Satan or a specific patron demon. It often involves a written declaration, sometimes signed in blood, committing the practitioner irrevocably to the Left-Hand Path.
figures.facet: Far from the Faustian folklore of 'selling one's soul' for earthly riches, the modern theistic pact is an act of spiritual self-ownership and mutual respect with the Demonic, rejecting submission entirely.
figures.provenance: S. Connolly, 'The Complete Book of Demonolatry' (2006); historical witch trial confessions re-appropriated.

figures.gloss: In the misanthropic currents of Traditional Satanism, this is the boundless, wrathful void that predates the restrictive, flawed material universe. It is the primal, dissolving force antagonistic to cosmic order.
figures.facet: It posits that the structured universe is an agonizing aberration. The spiritual mandate is not to conquer or harmonize with the world, but to aid in its magical dissolution back into formless infinity.
figures.provenance: Temple of the Black Light, 'Liber Azerate' (2002); Current 218 occult literature.

figures.gloss: The vast hierarchy of demonic spirits, heavily drawn from the Lesser Key of Solomon and ancient pantheons, approached as distinct gods, teachers, and powerful familiars who aid the practitioner's ascent.
figures.facet: Practitioners completely discard the Abrahamic names of God historically used by magicians to threaten and bind these spirits, instead evoking them through mutual respect, reciprocal offerings, and kinship.
figures.provenance: 'Ars Goetia' (17th century), radically revised by modern traditional demonolaters to remove Solomonic coercion.

figures.gloss: The ultimate teleological goal of the practitioner: to refine and elevate the individual consciousness until it becomes an independent, self-sustaining divine entity, entirely separate from the natural universe and its creator.
figures.facet: It represents the absolute rejection of traditional mysticism's goal (ego-death and union with the divine). It is the eternal, defiant, and isolated preservation of the 'I'.
figures.provenance: Stephen Flowers, 'Lords of the Left-Hand Path' (1997); Temple of Set's concept of Xeper.

figures.gloss: The practice of worshipping and working with demons as pre-Christian divine intelligences to be honored and allied with, rather than Solomonic spirits to be threatened and bound; codified in its modern organized form by S. Connolly and allied authors, with contested claims of generational lineage.
figures.facet: It is the current the Pact and Goetia entries already paraphrase without naming — the largest organized wing of theistic Satanic practice, distinguished from Church-of-Satan-style symbolic atheism by treating the demonic as literally real and owed reciprocal respect.
figures.provenance: S. Connolly, 'The Complete Book of Demonolatry' (2006) and the wider Demonolatry Practitioners' network literature of the 1990s-2000s.

figures.gloss: The most prolific living author of the modern adversarial current; founder of the Order of Phosphorus and later the Greater Church of Lucifer / Assembly of Light Bearers, and author of 'The Bible of the Adversary' (2007) and dozens of grimoires shaping current theistic-Satanic and Luciferian practice.
figures.facet: He is the tradition's bridge figure rather than a purist: his work moves fluidly between Satan, Lucifer, and the Draconian current, showing how porous the boundary is between theistic Satanism and its sibling Left-Hand Path movements in practice.
figures.provenance: Michael W. Ford, 'The Bible of the Adversary' (2007) and subsequent works; cited in this roster's own Lilith entry.

figures.gloss: Demonolatress and author of 'The Complete Book of Demonolatry' (2006), the standard modern manual of respectful demon-worship, offerings, and pact-making, and a principal systematizer of the framework this roster's rite entries already draw on.
figures.facet: Her project reframes centuries of coercive Solomonic grimoire technique as reciprocal alliance-craft, supplying the vocabulary of consent and mutual respect that separates modern Demonolatry from its exorcistic and goetic ancestors.
figures.provenance: S. Connolly, 'The Complete Book of Demonolatry' (2006); cited in this roster's own Pact entry.

figures.gloss: The Swedish anti-cosmic order, formerly the Misanthropic Luciferian Order, that produced 'Liber Azerate' (2002); its '218 current' theologizes chaos-gnosticism, casting the material cosmos as a prison to be magically dissolved back into primordial anti-cosmic Chaos.
figures.facet: It gives the anti-cosmic current an organized institutional body and a documented transmission route into the black metal underground (via members' involvement in bands including Dissection), rather than leaving 'Anti-Cosmic Chaos' as a free-floating idea with no historical carrier.
figures.provenance: Temple of the Black Light, 'Liber Azerate' (2002); cited in this roster's own Anti-Cosmic Chaos entry.

edges.note: Intra-tradition: Satan is the bestower of the Black Flame, the catalyst that initiates the practitioner's spiritual evolution.
Intra-tradition: Often worshipped together as the masculine and feminine pillars of demonic liberation and antinomian rebellion.
Intra-tradition: In anti-cosmic currents, Satan is viewed as a direct emanation or active agent of the primordial Chaos within the cosmos.
Intra-tradition: The pact is the operational mechanism by which a practitioner formalizes an alliance with specific Goetic spirits.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Explicit theological reversal; Satan is worshipped as the liberating hero, while Yahweh is condemned as the tyrannical cosmic enslaver.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both are cosmic rebels who defied a supreme creator deity to gift forbidden knowledge and self-awareness to humanity.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The adversarial framework of Satan heavily derives from Zoroastrianism's Angra Mainyu, though Theistic Satanism inverses the moral polarity.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both are nocturnal, adversarial figures who employ temptation, disruption, and forbidden magic to test and strengthen humanity.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both represent sovereign, formidable, and untamed aspects of the feminine divine dwelling in the underworld, entirely autonomous from patriarchal deities.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The mythological roots of Lilith trace back directly to the Lilitu demons associated with Inanna in ancient Mesopotamian religion.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both posit an inherent, transcendent fragment of divinity within humanity, though Satanism seeks to isolate and empower it rather than return it to a monad.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both conceptualize a distinct, personal spiritual energy or magical volition that can be cultivated and exerted upon the world.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Anti-Cosmic Satanism views the material creation as a prison built by a blind demiurge (mirroring Yaldabaoth) and seeks its violent dissolution.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A structural parallel where practitioners work intimately with powerful, morally ambiguous spirits housed in specific vessels or sigils to enact material change.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Radically opposed spiritual goals; Buddhism seeks the dissolution of the self (Anatman), whereas Left-Hand Path apotheosis seeks the eternal preservation of the ego.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both traditions envision the ultimate spiritual state as a permanent, individualized, and autonomous consciousness, rather than merging back into a universal source.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A loose parallel of a specialized practitioner actively crossing boundaries to forge binding, reciprocal alliances with potent otherworldly intelligences.

a documented lineage — the Talmudic night-demon (Eruvin 100b) and Ben Sira's rebel first-wife are the explicit source material modern Satanism's 'Dark Mother' reworks into a figure of sacred autonomy.
Intra-tradition: the Pact is Demonolatry's operational rite of formal alliance, drawn directly from Connolly's codified framework.
Intra-tradition: Demonolatry supplies the worship-not-binding stance that lets the Goetic hierarchy be approached as gods and allies rather than spirits coerced by Solomonic threat.
Intra-tradition: Connolly is Demonolatry's principal modern systematizer, named as such in this roster's own Pact provenance.
Intra-tradition: the Pact entry's provenance already cites Connolly's 'Complete Book of Demonolatry' by name.
Intra-tradition: the Lilith entry's own provenance already cites Ford's 'Bible of the Adversary' by name.

Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Ford founded the Luciferian Greater Church of Lucifer / Assembly of Light Bearers and writes across both currents — a documented historical bridge figure connecting theistic Satanism and Luciferianism rather than a mere structural parallel.
Intra-tradition: the Anti-Cosmic Chaos entry's own provenance already cites 'Liber Azerate' by name; the Temple of the Black Light is that text's producing order.

Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: both frame the structured material cosmos as the flawed or hostile product of a lesser, blind power, against which the initiate's work is directed — chaos-gnostic anti-cosmicism rhyming with Luciferian anti-demiurge doctrine, independently articulated currents.

Same Jewish-folkloric Lilith at different ontological rank: Luciferianism holds her as an emancipatory 'force' of antinomian feminine autonomy, while theistic Satanism enthrones her as a literal, co-equal Dark Mother goddess and consort of Satan.
Same referent at opposite ontological standing: Christianity's cast-out cosmic enemy is, in theistic Satanism, a real, sentient being actively communed with and worshipped, not merely opposed.

Direct documented repurposing, stated in the corpus's own provenance: Anton LaVey's 'Black Flame' philosophical metaphor for human self-awareness is literalized by Michael Aquino as Set's deliberate evolutionary gift, then re-adopted by theistic Satanists as the divine spark Satan himself bestows.

dossier.essence: A diffuse family of modern Western currents — coalescing from the 1970s and gaining definition in the 1990s-2000s — that venerate Satan/Lucifer and other demonic figures as real, sentient beings, in explicit contrast to Anton LaVey's atheistic, symbolic Satanism. It has no central church, spanning devotional Luciferianism, 'traditional' Demonolatry, and the misanthropic Anti-Cosmic or 'Current 218' schools. Its shared claim is that Satan is a genuine adversary-liberator who grants humanity forbidden knowledge, autonomy, and the potential for self-deification.
dossier.cosmos: Cosmologies vary widely. Devotional and Luciferian currents cast Satan as a benevolent bringer of the 'Black Flame' — the divine spark of individual consciousness — against a tyrannical creator, and aim at apotheosis, the eternal elevation of the self. Anti-cosmic currents invert Gnostic dualism, treating the ordered material universe as a prison and revering the primordial Chaos (Azerate) that will dissolve it; here the mandate is the world's undoing rather than harmony with it.
dossier.practice: Practice is largely solitary or in small covens and is autodidactic and text-driven: personal devotion, the evocation of Goetic demons approached as respected allies rather than bound servants, formal pacts of self-dedication, and shadow-work. There is no shared clergy, liturgy, or ethic, and stances range from reverent devotion to transgressive antinomianism.
dossier.canon: No shared canon; influential modern texts include Michael W. Ford's Luciferian works, S. Connolly's 'The Complete Book of Demonolatry' (2006), and the Temple of the Black Light's 'Liber Azerate' (2002), alongside the reappropriated grimoire the 'Ars Goetia' (part of the 17th-century 'Lesser Key of Solomon').
dossier.reflects: The moral inversion of the adversary — the impulse to redeem the cosmic rebel, reading the tempter of orthodoxy as the true liberator of the human self.
sources: Ruben van Luijk, 'Children of Lucifer: The Origins of Modern Religious Satanism' (2016)
Asbjorn Dyrendal, James R. Lewis & Jesper Aa. Petersen, 'The Invention of Satanism' (2016)
Per Faxneld & Jesper Aa. Petersen (eds.), 'The Devil's Party: Satanism in Modernity' (2013)
